permitSeason: Climbing permit required for glacier travel or above ~10,000 ft; wilderness reservations via Recreation.gov May–September
safetyNote: Alpine starts are standard on the Disappointment Cleaver route. Parties usually leave high camp at night or in the early morning to move through crevassed terrain and rockfall-prone sections before daytime warming increases hazard. Turnaround times should be firm, especially if weather, route conditions, or team pace deteriorate.

permitSeason: May 1–November 1 (quota lottery); year-round wilderness permit required
safetyNote: A pre-dawn start from Trail Camp, often around 4 AM, is strongly recommended for summit-day hikers. The upper mountain is fully exposed to afternoon thunderstorms and high winds. Experienced parties prioritize an early summit and descent.

permitSeason: Free wilderness permit year-round; $25 summit pass above 10,000 ft (3 days) or $30 annual pass
safetyNote: Avalanche Gulch is often climbed on firm spring snow, when crampons and ice axe are standard equipment. Start early, ascend while the snow is firm, and descend before afternoon warming increases rockfall, wet-slide, and soft-snow hazards. In dry late-season conditions, expect loose scree, increased rockfall exposure, and slow travel.

permitSeason: No climbing permit required for standard summer day hikes
safetyNote: The upper mountain is broad, open, and highly exposed to lightning. Afternoon thunderstorms are common in July and August, so most parties start before dawn and aim to be descending from the summit area by late morning or midday.

permitSeason: No dedicated climbing permit; recreation/parking pass required at Heliotrope Ridge trailhead
permitFee: No climbing permit fee; parking pass required
safetyNote: An alpine start is strongly recommended. Cooler morning conditions often provide firmer travel and reduce warming-related wet-snow hazards, while also improving crampon purchase on the Roman Wall approach. Afternoon conditions on the lower Coleman Glacier can become slushy, and crevasse-fall risk increases as snow bridges soften.

permitSeason: Free self-issue day-use wilderness permit required; $5/person overnight quota permit
permitFee: Free day-use permit; tram ticket additional
safetyNote: The temperature differential between Palm Springs and the summit is extreme, often reaching 30 to 40 degrees Fahrenheit cooler at the peak. Afternoon thunderstorms are common during summer, particularly during monsoonal patterns in July and August, and pose serious risk on exposed terrain above 9,500 feet. Winter and shoulder-season conditions may require traction devices such as microspikes or crampons.

permitSeason: Free wilderness permit required for all day and overnight trips via SGWA; Adventure Pass required for trailhead parking
safetyNote: Alpine starts before dawn are strongly recommended from July through September so hikers can summit early and descend below exposed ridge terrain before afternoon thunderstorms develop.

permitSeason: No permit required for day hiking or backpacking in Mount Charleston Wilderness
safetyNote: The upper South Loop route is exposed to wind, lightning, and rapid temperature change, while the lower trailhead area can be hot in summer. Start early, carry all needed water, and plan to be off the exposed summit ridge before afternoon storms build.

permitSeason: Climbing pass required May 1–September 30 above 7,000 ft ($20/person via Recreation.gov); $5 day-use at Cold Springs trailhead
safetyNote: Afternoon thunderstorms are common in summer on Mount Adams. Plan firm turnaround times, carry layers for rapid weather changes, and aim to descend before storm activity intensifies. The South Spur is best climbed when significant snow covers the route; without snow, the upper approach becomes loose, unpleasant scree. Glissading is a common descent on soft snow but requires self-arrest skill; avoid glissading on firm or icy snow where a fall cannot be arrested.

bestRoute: Hoh River Trail to Blue Glacier
permitSeason: Wilderness permits required May 15–October 15; summer permits released April 15 via Recreation.gov
safetyNote: Crevasse Hazard: Blue Glacier is subject to significant annual change due to glacier recession. Crevasse patterns shift yearly; bridged crevasses collapse without warning. Rope teams must maintain constant awareness and be prepared to reassess the route during each crossing.

permitSeason: Mount Hood Climbing Permit required year-round above 9,500 ft via Recreation.gov
safetyNote: An alpine start is standard on Mount Hood. Most parties climb overnight or before dawn to complete descent before afternoon warming increases rockfall, wet-snow, and snow-bridge hazards. Turnaround times should be firm, especially if weather, visibility, or snow conditions deteriorate.

permitSeason: June–October; no day-use permit required
safetyNote: An early start is strongly recommended. Afternoon thunderstorms are common during summer, and the summit ridge is fully exposed to lightning and high winds. Parties that delay departure risk sudden weather forcing retreat near the summit.

relatedLinks.label: Wheeler Peak trip report
relatedLinks.description: For firsthand route notes, campground logistics, Stella Lake, and summit-day pacing, read the Wilderness Beta Wheeler Peak trip report.

permitSeason: No quota climbing permit; sign trailhead register. Northwest Forest Pass or federal interagency pass required at North Fork Sauk Trailhead
safetyNote: Glacier travel demands proficiency with crevasse rescue, self-arrest, and roped team movement. Parties without recent glacier-travel practice should build time into the itinerary for review of rope systems, crevasse rescue, and self-arrest before the summit push.

bestRoute: South Face Route via Barcroft Gate
permitSeason: No day-hiking permit required; verify current road, gate, and fire restrictions
safetyNote: Water is not available on the mountain. Hikers should carry sufficient water capacity for the entire ascent and descent. Afternoon thunderstorms are possible from July through September. Extreme winds have been recorded on the mountain in severe weather, and even ordinary summit-day winds can be exhausting on the exposed ridge. Temperature can drop 30 degrees or more from the adjacent valley floor.

permitSeason: Open for individual climbers; group-use visitor permit may be required for organized, commercial, or stock-supported parties
safetyNote: Alpine start (midnight to 5 AM) is highly recommended to secure stable snow conditions, minimize crevasse hazard, and allow time to manage bergschrund crossing before afternoon warming weakens snow bridges.

bestRoute: West Tensleep Trail to Mistymoon Lake
permitSeason: Free self-issued wilderness registration at trailhead; no climbing fee
safetyNote: An early alpine start from Mistymoon Lake is strongly recommended to reduce exposure to afternoon thunderstorms during the summit push and descent through the boulder field.

permitSeason: No climbing permit for day use; backcountry permit required for overnight camping (quotas apply for Garnet Canyon and Lower Saddle)
permitFee: No day-use climbing fee; backcountry permit fee for overnight
safetyNote: Route-finding on the descent from the Upper Saddle is a common source of error on this route, and afternoon storms can make the upper mountain dangerous quickly. Parties should confirm the descent line before committing to the upper route and plan to be off exposed terrain before thunderstorm development.

permitSeason: No climbing permit required; verify current Custer Gallatin National Forest food-storage and wilderness regulations
safetyNote: Keen route-finding and proper gear are essential on Granite Peak. Parties should study route descriptions before departure and carry maps, navigation tools, and backup navigation methods. Joe Josephson's Montana's Highest Point: A Climbing Guide & Map is a commonly cited dedicated guide for the standard route.

bestRoute: Hurricane Creek Trail / Thorp Creek East Ridge
permitSeason: Free self-issue Eagle Cap Wilderness permit at trailhead; Northwest Forest Pass or America the Beautiful Pass required for Hurricane Creek Trailhead parking
safetyNote: No trail exists above treeline. Route-finding on the east ridge requires map and compass or GPS; multiple cairn lines, social paths, and game trails can mislead climbers in low visibility. An early start is strongly recommended to clear the exposed upper ridge before afternoon thunderstorms can develop during summer.

permitSeason: July through September; free sign-in kiosk at Henry's Fork Trailhead
safetyNote: Afternoon thunderstorms are common in July and August. Start early and plan to be off the exposed summit ridge before storms develop. Lightning is a significant hazard.

relatedLinks.label: Kings Peak trip report
relatedLinks.description: For a firsthand Henry's Fork itinerary with Dollar Lake camp notes, Gunsight Pass route choices, and summit-day timing, read the Wilderness Beta Kings Peak trip report.

bestRoute: High Peak Road (summit closed to public)
permitSeason: Summit closed to public; special authorization required for legal entry
permitFee: No standard public summit access; observatory tours may be available seasonally
callout: Mount Graham is the awkward one. The summit is one of the 57 lower-48 ultras and, by prominence logic, a mandatory objective. Physically, the route is not mysterious: the upper service-road corridor beyond the public closure leads toward the summit area, and the terrain is essentially a high-elevation road walk. Legally, that same corridor enters the Mount Graham Red Squirrel Refugium, where public access is restricted to protect endangered-species habitat.
safetyNote: Unauthorized entry into the Mount Graham Red Squirrel Refugium can carry federal consequences. The closure exists because the summit area overlaps protected habitat for the Mount Graham red squirrel. Mount Graham is ultimately an ethics-and-risk decision rather than a routefinding challenge.

permitSeason: Parking or federal recreation pass required at Manker Flats; free wilderness permit required for designated wilderness entry
permitFee: Parking/day-use fee at Manker Flats; verify current pass rules with Angeles National Forest
safetyNote: Afternoon thunderstorms develop rapidly over the summit July through August. The exposed Backbone ridge offers no shelter; parties should plan to be off the ridge by midday during monsoon season.

permitSeason: Day hikes require no permit; voluntary backcountry permits for overnight trips; verify current NPS rules and road conditions
permitFee: Free; Death Valley entrance fee may apply
safetyNote: The upper ridge provides little shelter and is exposed to strong winds, rapid weather changes, and occasional summer thunderstorms. Start early, especially in warm weather or unsettled forecasts, and avoid being on the summit ridge if storms are building.

bestRoute: Burlfriends Trail from La Sal Pass
permitSeason: No permit required for climbing or dispersed camping; confirm current Manti-La Sal NF fire and road rules
safetyNote: Snow can persist in shaded gullies and on north-facing slopes into early or mid-summer. June and early-July ascents may require crampons, an ice axe, and the ability to self-arrest if firm snow remains in the upper gully. Water sources above treeline are very limited; carry adequate supply from the trailhead.

permitSeason: No climbing permit for day hiking; parking, recreation pass, and facility fees vary by trailhead (AMC/WMNF)
permitFee: Free; parking, recreation pass, or facility fees may apply by trailhead
safetyNote: Tuckerman Ravine and nearby terrain are subject to avalanche forecasts, General Bulletins, and seasonal closures or route restrictions when snow, ice, undermined snow, waterfall holes, or avalanche conditions create unacceptable hazard. In spring, specific sections such as the Lip area of the Tuckerman Ravine Trail may be closed until snow has melted out. Check Mount Washington Avalanche Center and Pinkham Notch guidance before entering the ravine.

permitSeason: No climbing permit required; Black Mountain Campground closed 2025–2026 (reopens 2027)
safetyNote: Above 5,000 feet, fog and wind exposure increase markedly, and travel can feel significantly more severe than lower sections despite remaining on trail. Dense fog, cold rain, and strong winds can occur in any season; carry insulating layers and a waterproof shell.

permitSeason: No permit required for day use; verify winter road access and any seasonal requirements with Coconino National Forest
safetyNote: Afternoon thunderstorms build rapidly during the July through September monsoon season, with lightning posing a documented fatality risk on the exposed upper ridge. During monsoon season, many parties start at or before dawn and aim to be off the exposed ridge before noon.

permitSeason: No climbing permit required; primitive first-come trailhead campground at Borah Peak Trailhead
safetyNote: A very early start, often 3–4 AM, is strongly recommended in summer. Plan to summit early and be descending well before afternoon thunderstorm development, which is common July through August. The exposed ridge presents significant lightning risk.

bestRoute: Jefferson Summit Access to South Summit
permitSeason: No permit generally required; confirm current fire, road, and wilderness restrictions
safetyNote: High altitude near 12,000 feet combined with rapid elevation gain demands proper acclimatization. Parties unaccustomed to altitude above 10,000 feet should allow extra time and descend if symptoms of acute mountain sickness develop. Summer thunderstorms are common in July and August; plan morning starts and be prepared to descend quickly if storms threaten.

bestRoute: Bull Creek Pass to Mount Ellen North Ridge
permitSeason: No climbing permit required; BLM-managed public land
safetyNote: The false summit near 11,400 feet appears terminal and may initially seem to be the highest point. Continue past it; the true summit lies beyond and is distinguished by a mailbox registry.

bestRoute: Mill Fork Trail from Loop Campground
permitSeason: No climbing permit required; campground fees apply at Loop Campground
safetyNote: False summits near the peak can create navigation hazards on descent. Before descending, identify the main summit cairn carefully to avoid drifting onto adjacent ridge systems or following false summit terrain. Cloud cover can shroud summit areas while surrounding desert basins remain clear, reducing visibility for route-finding.

permitSeason: Central Cascades Wilderness Permit required June 15–October 15: $1/person day use, $6/group overnight via Recreation.gov; federal recreation pass required at Pamelia Lake Trailhead
callout: Route character changes substantially with snowpack. Late June to mid-July is generally preferred when snowfields are consolidated and firm. Late-season conditions on bare rock involve sections reported as low 5th class. Soft afternoon snow on the traverse has contributed to serious accidents; an early start from high camp is standard practice. Check current trip reports and ranger-district notices before committing to the climb. This route description is not a substitute for a map, GPS track, current conditions report, and competent alpine route-finding.

permitSeason: No climbing permit or fee; BLM-managed public land
safetyNote: The route is exceptionally steep with minimal relief. Afternoon wind on the exposed ridge can rapidly increase objective danger; an early morning start is strongly recommended. Water is extremely scarce along the route; carry substantial capacity or pre-position water.

permitSeason: No climbing permit required; Half Moon Campground fee may apply
safetyNote: Rockfall hazard exists on loose scree sections, particularly where parties travel on the same slope. Avoid positioning directly above or below other climbers, move efficiently through scree, and consider wearing a helmet when multiple groups share the route.

bestRoute: Red Horn Lane / Ashley Lakes Approach toward Duncan Lake
permitSeason: Flathead Reservation Conservation License required; McDonald Peak Grizzly Bear Closure Area closed July 15–October 1 unless posted otherwise
permitFee: $80 three-day Conservation License; $100 annual; $100 Camping License for overnight
safetyNote: The McDonald Peak Grizzly Bear Closure Area is closed to all recreational use from July 15 through October 1 each year unless posted otherwise. Do not attempt the climb during this closure window. The practical climbing season is generally early June through July 14, depending on snowpack, road access, and posted tribal regulations. Confirm closure status, access conditions, and all permit requirements with the Confederated Salish and Kootenai Tribes before entering the area.

permitSeason: Central Cascades Wilderness Permit required June 15–October 15: $1/person day use, $6/group overnight via Recreation.gov; Northwest Forest Pass or America the Beautiful Pass required at Devil's Lake Trailhead
safetyNote: The upper mountain above treeline is fully exposed. Afternoon convective storms are common around South Sister from July through August. Most experienced parties aim to reach the summit by midday and clear the crater rim by early afternoon to stay ahead of lightning risk.

relatedLinks.label: South Sister permit system
relatedLinks.description: For current release windows, quota strategy, and day-use versus overnight rules, see the Wilderness Beta guide to the South Sister permit system.

permitSeason: Tribal land access; verify current Mescalero Apache, Ski Apache, and Lincoln National Forest requirements
permitFee: Varies; verify current access, tribal, and resort requirements
safetyNote: Past forest fires have left fire-scarred slopes on upper sections of the route with increased rockfall hazard from loose, unstable rock. Beyond Lookout Mountain, the route fades and requires careful map and compass navigation on exposed terrain where route-finding becomes a significant consideration.

permitSeason: No climbing permit required; Barr Trailhead parking fee typically $25–$32.50 per day (reserve in peak season)
permitFee: Free (no climbing permit; trailhead parking fee applies)
safetyNote: Afternoon thunderstorms are common in summer, and the upper route is exposed above treeline. Start early, set a firm turnaround time, and aim to be descending from the summit area by late morning or midday. Barr Camp can be a useful place to ask about recent conditions, but hikers should make independent weather decisions.

bestRoute: North Peak Trail from North Mount Nebo Trailhead
permitSeason: No permit required for day hiking or overnight travel in Mount Nebo Wilderness
safetyNote: Loose shale and black limestone on the final ridge create rockfall hazard in parties. Members should maintain separation on the upper pitch and avoid stopping beneath other climbers on the ascent.

permitSeason: No climbing permit required; bear-resistant food storage required under Kootenai NF rules
safetyNote: The crux Class 4 chimney features moderate exposure and loose limestone; a rope team should be prepared for rapid weather deterioration on the exposed upper ridge. Descent of the crux and rocky creek-bed descent can be hazardous; a rope is strongly recommended for parties considering the descent route.

permitSeason: No day-hiking permit; Timber Creek Campground reservations and fees may apply
safetyNote: Timber Creek Road access can vary due to maintenance, seasonal conditions, or temporary closures. Verify access with the Ely Ranger District at 775-289-3031 before traveling to the trailhead.

permitSeason: No permit required; verify current BLM road access and conditions
safetyNote: El Dorado Canyon Road is rough and boulder-strewn, especially in the upper sections. High-clearance four-wheel drive should be the planning baseline. Lower portions may be accessible to standard vehicles depending on conditions, but undercarriage damage is possible. Choose your parking point and vehicle accordingly.

permitSeason: No backpacking permit required; confirm current refuge rules, road closures, and fire restrictions
safetyNote: Check the FWS current conditions page before departure. As of the May 28, 2026 FWS update, Alamo Road was only partially open, with sections between Hidden Forest Road and the Dry Lake bed closed. Access from Corn Creek to Hidden Forest Road requires high-clearance and 4WD vehicles. Because the route is remote and emergency response may be significantly delayed, parties should be prepared for self-rescue and carry communication devices.

bestRoute: East Ridge via Birch Creek Valley
permitSeason: No climbing permit required; confirm current BLM and Caribou-Targhee NF camping and fire rules
safetyNote: Afternoon thunderstorms represent the primary hazard on the exposed ridge. Parties must plan to be off the summit by early afternoon, which typically necessitates a 5:00–6:00 AM start. The rapid elevation gain also demands adequate acclimatization; many parties spend a night at Birch Creek Campground or nearby alternatives before attempting the climb.

permitSeason: No wilderness permit; private landowner permission required — verify current access before attempting
safetyNote: Private land surrounds much of the approach and summit area. Current access permission must be independently verified with the appropriate landowner or land manager before any attempt. Stay on the approved route to avoid trespassing or entering restricted mining property.

permitSeason: No climbing permit required; Northwest Forest Pass or federal interagency pass required at Esmeralda Trailhead
permitFee: Northwest Forest Pass or equivalent required at trailhead
safetyNote: Rockfall is a serious overhead hazard in the Cascadian Couloir, especially when multiple parties are active. A climbing helmet should be considered essential. Route-finding on the upper mountain is complex and unmarked; carry a topographic map, GPS track, and the skills to navigate without relying on cairns alone. Early-season ascents may require an ice axe, crampons, and snow-climbing experience.

permitSeason: No permit for standard day hiking; verify current Forest Service camping and fire restrictions
safetyNote: Lake Como Road is extremely rough and impassable to standard passenger vehicles. Confirm vehicle capability before attempting to drive beyond the lower parking area. Snowmelt, runoff, washouts, and lingering snow can make sections of the road temporarily impassable, especially in June and early July.

permitSeason: No climbing permit required; timed Timpooneke trailhead parking on summer weekends and holidays via Recreation.gov
permitFee: Free (timed parking permit required on weekends/holidays)
safetyNote: An early start is strongly recommended, especially on weekends when parking fills early. Afternoon thunderstorms develop rapidly; plan a turnaround time that allows descent off the exposed ridge before early afternoon.

permitSeason: No permit required; verify BLM fire restrictions and road conditions before travel
safetyNote: Access is severely constrained by the Goshute Indian Reservation, which blocks western approaches; remain on the Granite Creek corridor and do not cross reservation lands without explicit permission. The area is extremely remote with no cell service and emergency response many hours away. Water availability decreases above 10,000 feet; carry sufficient capacity for long dry stretches.

permitSeason: Park entrance pass required year-round; Lassen Peak Trail typically open late June through September depending on snow and park road access
permitFee: Park entrance fee required; America the Beautiful Pass accepted
safetyNote: Start early and avoid electrical weather. Lassen Peak is exposed, windy, and above 10,000 feet. If thunderstorms are forecast or clouds are building, turn around before reaching the upper mountain.

permitSeason: Backcountry permit required for overnight itineraries; advance Recreation.gov reservations strongly recommended
permitFee: No climbing fee; entrance and backcountry permit fees apply
safetyNote: Route-finding is difficult on the Stoney Indian Peaks traverse, particularly along the exposed east-side section. Parties commonly lose the route in poor visibility. Descending in deteriorating weather on loose, unstable rock is hazardous. Early-season attempts can involve lingering snow on narrow ledges, increasing avalanche and slip risk.

permitSeason: No climbing permit or fee; free Columbine Campground trailhead access
safetyNote: Afternoon thunderstorms are common in the Toiyabe Range during summer. Start early and aim to be off the summit ridge and exposed high crest before storms develop. Much of the upper route is open terrain with limited shelter from lightning, wind, or sudden temperature drops.

permitSeason: No standard recreation permit; check current Forest Service regulations and seasonal closures
safetyNote: A rope may provide limited benefit on much of this route because protection can be unreliable in loose volcanic rock, and party movement can increase rockfall exposure. Route-finding is essential; parties should carry detailed maps and possess competency in cross-country navigation through complex volcanic terrain.

permitSeason: No permit required for day hiking on Colville National Forest land
safetyNote: Start early to reduce exposure to afternoon thunderstorms, which develop rapidly mid-summer over exposed alpine terrain where the summit offers minimal shelter.

permitSeason: No hiking permit required; Coronado National Forest or Interagency pass required at Marshall Gulch ($8 day-use on-site)
safetyNote: The summit is often 20 to 30 degrees Fahrenheit cooler than Tucson, especially during warm-season afternoons. Summer thunderstorms build rapidly on the ridgeline from July through September; plan to be off exposed terrain before midday.

permitSeason: No day-hiking permit; $8 day-use fee may apply at Rustler Park; confirm current fee and pass exemptions
permitFee: $8 day-use at Rustler Park (verify; federal passes may apply)
safetyNote: Afternoon thunderstorms are common during monsoon season (July–August). Black bears inhabit the Chiricahua region; use proper food storage and bear boxes at developed sites for overnight trips.

permitSeason: Self-issue wilderness permit for overnight camping; California campfire permit required for stoves and fires
safetyNote: Mount Eddy's remote location at the crest of the Trinity Divide exposes hikers to rapid weather changes. Summer afternoon thunderstorms are common from July through September. Early-season ascents in June may encounter significant snow above 8,500 feet. Afternoon winds can be severe at the summit.

bestRoute: Huachuca Crest Trail from Montezuma Pass
permitSeason: No permit required for day hiking; confirm current Coronado NF camping and fire rules
safetyNote: The area's proximity to the Mexico border makes situational awareness important. Standard hiking routes are commonly used by recreationists, but awareness of the border security context is advisable.
